title: Hypertrophy happens across a wide rep range

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Grgic J, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Strength and hypertrophy adaptations between low- vs. high-load resistance training. J Strength Cond Res 2017;31(12):3508–3523

body:
**Claim:** Muscle can be built across a broad loading spectrum — roughly 5–30 reps per set —
as long as sets are taken close to failure. There is no single "hypertrophy rep range."

**Evidence:** A meta-analysis found similar hypertrophy between low-load (>15 rep) and
high-load (<15 rep) training when sets approached failure; load mattered for maximal
strength but not for growth (Schoenfeld 2017).

**Takeaway:** Choose reps by practicality and joint comfort, not dogma. The 8–15 range is
convenient for most accessory work (enough load to be efficient, enough reps to be safe and
controllable), but 5–8 and 15–30 both grow muscle when effort is high. This gives the coach
freedom to prescribe lighter, higher-rep work when an injury note or low readiness makes
heavy loading unwise, without sacrificing hypertrophy.

title: Full range of motion and training at long muscle lengths

sources.ref: Ottinger CR, et al. Greater gastrocnemius muscle hypertrophy after partial range of motion training performed at long muscle lengths. J Strength Cond Res 2023;37(6):1231–1237

body:
**Claim:** Training through a full range of motion — and especially loading the muscle in
its lengthened (stretched) position — tends to produce equal or greater hypertrophy than
short, partial reps done in the shortened position.

**Evidence:** Partial reps performed at long muscle lengths produced greater gastrocnemius
growth than full ROM, and clearly more than shortened-position partials, in a controlled
trial (Ottinger 2023); this aligns with a broader body of ROM research favouring the
stretched position.

**Takeaway:** Cue a full, controlled range with an emphasis on the deep/stretched portion
(e.g. a deep stretch on RDLs, dips, calf raises, incline curls). When time or fatigue forces
partials, bias them toward the lengthened end. The coach can flag consistently short ROM as
a likely reason a muscle is lagging despite adequate set counts.

title: Diminishing returns and junk volume past the productive range

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci 2017;35(11):1073–1082

body:
**Claim:** Adding volume helps only up to a point. Past a muscle's productive range, extra
sets yield little to no additional growth ("junk volume") while adding fatigue that can
impair recovery and subsequent sessions.

**Evidence:** The volume dose-response curve flattens at higher weekly set counts; the
incremental gain per set shrinks as volume climbs, and the highest volumes carry recovery
costs that can offset benefit (Schoenfeld 2017 and subsequent meta-regressions).

**Takeaway:** More is not always better. The app's `MUSCLE_RANGES` encode an upper bound
per muscle; the weekly-volume view flags a muscle as `OVER` when sets exceed it. When a
muscle is already at or above its range, the coach should not prescribe still more volume —
redistribute sets to lagging muscles, or improve set quality (load, proximity to failure)
instead.

title: Heavy loads are specific to maximal strength

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Grgic J, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Strength and hypertrophy adaptations between low- vs. high-load resistance training. J Strength Cond Res 2017;31(12):3508–3523
American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708

body:
**Claim:** Maximal strength (1RM) is best developed with heavy loads — roughly ≥80% 1RM,
in the 1–6 rep range — because strength adaptations are specific to the load and skill of
lifting heavy.

**Evidence:** While hypertrophy is load-agnostic, the same meta-analysis found clearly
greater 1RM gains from high-load training (Schoenfeld 2017); ACSM's position stand
recommends heavy loading for advanced strength progression (ACSM 2009).

**Takeaway:** Match load to goal. If the user's goal is strength, program the main lifts
heavy (low reps, longer rest, higher effort). If the goal is size, load can be flexible.
This distinction should shape the coach's weight prescriptions — an e1RM-derived %1RM for
strength intent, a wider load window for hypertrophy intent — and is why the app tracks
estimated 1RM per exercise as the anchor for prescribing weights.

title: Mechanical tension is the primary driver of hypertrophy

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ. The mechanisms of muscle hypertrophy and their application to resistance training. J Strength Cond Res 2010;24(10):2857–2872
Damas F, et al. J Physiol 2016;594(18):5209–5222

body:
**Claim:** High mechanical tension applied to a muscle through a challenging range of
motion is the dominant stimulus for hypertrophy. Metabolic stress and muscle damage are
secondary contributors, not requirements.

**Evidence:** The classic three-mechanism model (Schoenfeld 2010) identifies mechanical
tension, metabolic stress, and muscle damage — but subsequent work shows tension is
primary: hypertrophy tracks with hard, tension-producing sets, while damage is not the
mediator of growth (Damas 2016).

**Takeaway:** Program hard sets with meaningful load and full effort — that is what
generates the tension that drives growth. Chasing "the pump" or soreness for their own sake
is misguided; they are byproducts, not goals. This is why the coach should judge a session
by hard working sets and progressive load, not by how sore or fatigued it felt.

title: Metabolic stress is a secondary contributor

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ. The mechanisms of muscle hypertrophy and their application to resistance training. J Strength Cond Res 2010;24(10):2857–2872

body:
**Claim:** Metabolic stress (the "burn"/pump from accumulated metabolites in higher-rep,
shorter-rest sets) can contribute to hypertrophy, but it is secondary to mechanical tension
and not a target in its own right.

**Evidence:** Schoenfeld's three-mechanism model lists metabolic stress alongside tension
and damage; later evidence positions it as a modest, tension-dependent contributor rather
than an independent driver (Schoenfeld 2010).

**Takeaway:** Higher-rep, shorter-rest "pump" work is a reasonable tool — especially for
smaller muscles and machine/isolation movements where loading heavy is awkward — but it
complements, not replaces, heavier tension-producing work. The coach shouldn't prescribe
burn-focused work as a substitute for progressive overload on the main lifts.

title: How a training session turns into muscle

sources.ref: Damas F, et al. J Physiol 2016;594(18):5209–5222
Areta JL, et al. Timing and distribution of protein ingestion during prolonged recovery from resistance exercise alters myofibrillar protein synthesis. J Physiol 2013;591(9):2319–2331

body:
**Claim:** A resistance session elevates muscle protein synthesis (MPS) for roughly 24–48 h
afterward; growth is the net result of many such elevations exceeding breakdown over weeks,
supported by adequate protein intake.

**Evidence:** MPS rises after training and, once early-program damage subsides, correlates
with hypertrophy (Damas 2016); protein feeding across the recovery window modulates the MPS
response (Areta 2013).

**Takeaway:** Growth is cumulative and slow — the outcome of consistent hard sessions plus
sufficient protein, not any single workout. This frames realistic expectations: the coach
should point to multi-week trends (e1RM, volume) rather than session-to-session noise, and
reinforce that the elevated-MPS window is best "fed" by hitting daily protein, not by any
magic post-workout timing (see nutrition cards).

title: Muscle damage and soreness are not the goal

sources.ref: Damas F, et al. Resistance training-induced changes in integrated myofibrillar protein synthesis are related to hypertrophy only after attenuation of muscle damage. J Physiol 2016;594(18):5209–5222

body:
**Claim:** Muscle damage — and the soreness (DOMS) that often accompanies it — is a
side effect of training, not a driver of growth. You do not need to be sore to have trained
productively.

**Evidence:** Early-program muscle protein synthesis was directed at *repairing* damage
rather than building new tissue; hypertrophy tracked with protein synthesis only once damage
was attenuated (Damas 2016). Soreness reflects unaccustomed stimulus, not superior stimulus.

**Takeaway:** Don't chase soreness or judge a session by how wrecked it felt. Novel
exercises cause more DOMS without more growth. The coach should reassure users that reduced
soreness over weeks is normal adaptation, not lost progress, and should not add volume or
novelty just to "feel it more." Judge progress by load, reps, and volume trends instead.

title: Progressive overload drives long-term adaptation

sources.ref: American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708
Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci 2017;35(11):1073–1082

body:
**Claim:** Muscles adapt to the demands placed on them, so the training stimulus must
gradually increase over time. Progression can come from more load, more reps, more sets, or
better technique/range — not load alone.

**Evidence:** ACSM's progression position stand establishes systematically increasing
demand as the basis for continued gains; adding volume over time is one validated lever
(Schoenfeld 2017), alongside load and rep progression.

**Takeaway:** When last session's targets were hit cleanly, nudge the next session up —
add reps within the target range, then add load when the top of the range is reached (double
progression). This is precisely the rule the app's workout creator follows: "nudge
weights/reps up where the last comparable session hit its targets." The coach should ground
progression suggestions in the user's own last-session numbers, not generic increments.

title: Proximity to failure and reps in reserve

sources.ref: Refalo MC, et al. Influence of resistance training proximity-to-failure on skeletal muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review with meta-analysis. Sports Med 2023;53(3):649–665

body:
**Claim:** Sets should be taken close to failure to grow well, but training *to* momentary
failure on every set is unnecessary and adds fatigue. Roughly 0–3 reps in reserve (RIR)
captures most of the benefit.

**Evidence:** A meta-analysis found only a trivial hypertrophy advantage for training to
failure vs stopping short, and closer-to-failure training was associated with slightly
*smaller* strength gains and more fatigue (Refalo 2023).

**Takeaway:** Cue effort, not annihilation: most working sets should end around 1–3 RIR.
Reserve true failure for isolation work or the last set of an exercise. When readiness is
low, backing off to ~2–3 RIR is a sensible way to keep stimulus while cutting fatigue —
consistent with the app trimming intensity on low-readiness days.

title: Train each muscle about twice per week

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Sports Med 2016;46(11):1689–1697

body:
**Claim:** For a fixed weekly volume, training a muscle ~2×/week produces more hypertrophy
than hitting it all in one session. Frequency mostly matters as a tool for *distributing*
volume across sessions.

**Evidence:** A meta-analysis found that, when weekly sets are equated, twice-weekly
frequency beats once-weekly for growth; whether 3× beats 2× is unresolved (Schoenfeld
2016).

**Takeaway:** Spread each muscle's weekly sets over at least two sessions rather than one
punishing session. This keeps per-session volume in a recoverable range and lets each set
be higher quality. When the coach builds a split or a workout, it should ensure major
muscles recur ~2×/week across the cycle, not that any single day maximizes one muscle.

title: Weekly volume dose–response for hypertrophy

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci 2017;35(11):1073–1082

body:
**Claim:** Muscle growth rises with weekly hard-set volume in a graded, dose-response
fashion. Roughly 10–20 hard sets per muscle group per week drives near-maximal hypertrophy
for most trainees.

**Evidence:** A meta-analysis of 15 studies found a significant, graded effect of weekly
sets on muscle size — each additional weekly set was associated with a further increase in
growth, with a threshold around ~10 sets for near-maximal gains (Schoenfeld 2017).

**Takeaway:** Aim for ~10–20 direct working sets per muscle per week. This is exactly the
window the app already encodes in `MUSCLE_RANGES` (per-muscle optimal set ranges) — cards
and code agree. When weekly volume for a muscle sits below its range, adding sets is the
highest-yield change; the coach should surface under-trained muscles by name and nudge
volume up toward the range.

title: Creatine monohydrate: the proven supplement

sources.ref: Kreider RB, et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation in exercise, sport, and medicine. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017;14:18

body:
**Claim:** Creatine monohydrate is the most effective, best-evidenced supplement for
increasing high-intensity performance and lean mass over time. It is safe and well-tolerated
at ~3–5 g/day long term.

**Evidence:** The ISSN position stand concludes creatine monohydrate reliably increases
high-intensity exercise capacity and training-related lean mass, and is safe up to ~30 g/day
for years in healthy people (Kreider 2017).

**Takeaway:** A flat ~3–5 g/day (no loading phase needed) is a cheap, high-confidence
recommendation for almost anyone training for strength or size; benefits accrue as muscle
creatine stores saturate over ~2–4 weeks. Most other supplements have far weaker evidence.
The coach can recommend creatine confidently while treating flashier supplements skeptically.

title: Cut at ~0.5–1% bodyweight per week

sources.ref: Garthe I, et al. Effect of two different weight-loss rates on body composition and strength and power-related performance in elite athletes. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab 2011;21(2):97–104
Helms ER, Aragon AA, Fitschen PJ. Evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2014;11:20

body:
**Claim:** When losing fat while training, a moderate rate of ~0.5–1% of bodyweight per week
best preserves (or even builds) lean mass and strength. Faster loss increases muscle and
performance loss.

**Evidence:** A slower ~0.7%/week loss increased lean mass and 1RM strength, while a faster
~1.4%/week loss did not (Garthe 2011); Helms 2014 recommends ~0.5–1%/week for physique
athletes.

**Takeaway:** For a user cutting (e.g. toward a 72 kg goal), target a ~0.5–1%/week drop —
for an 80 kg person that's ~0.4–0.8 kg/week — via a modest deficit, high protein, and
continued heavy training. The coach should flag crash diets: losing faster sacrifices the
muscle and strength the training is meant to protect.

title: Energy balance drives weight direction

sources.ref: Longland TM, et al. Higher compared with lower dietary protein during an energy deficit combined with intense exercise promotes greater lean mass gain and fat mass loss: a randomized trial. Am J Clin Nutr 2016;103(3):738–746
Helms ER, Aragon AA, Fitschen PJ. Evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation: nutrition and supplementation. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2014;11:20

body:
**Claim:** Whether bodyweight rises or falls is governed by energy balance (calories in vs
out). Building muscle is easiest in a small surplus and losing fat requires a deficit, but
body *recomposition* (gaining muscle while losing fat) is possible — especially for
beginners, returnees, and higher-body-fat individuals — with high protein and hard training.

**Evidence:** In an aggressive deficit with high protein and intense training, participants
still gained lean mass while losing fat (Longland 2016); Helms 2014 frames calorie level as
the primary determinant of weight change.

**Takeaway:** Set calorie level to the primary goal — modest surplus to prioritize size,
deficit to prioritize leanness — while keeping protein high to protect or build muscle either
way. The coach should tie a user's stated goal (e.g. "cut to 72 kg") to an appropriate
energy-balance direction, not just training changes.

title: Lean bulk on a small surplus

sources.ref: Iraki J, Fitschen P, Espinar S, Helms E. Nutrition recommendations for bodybuilders in the off-season: a narrative review. Sports 2019;7(7):154

body:
**Claim:** To build muscle with minimal fat gain, a small energy surplus (~10–20% over
maintenance, ~200–500 kcal) is enough. Larger surpluses accelerate fat gain without
proportionally more muscle, since muscle growth is rate-limited.

**Evidence:** Off-season nutrition recommendations advise a modest surplus targeting
~0.25–0.5% bodyweight gain per week for novice/intermediate lifters, with advanced lifters
gaining even more slowly (Iraki 2019).

**Takeaway:** "Dirty bulking" mostly adds fat you'll later have to cut. Aim for a slow gain
(~0.25–0.5%/week), keep protein at target, and let training drive the muscle. The coach
should temper expectations: muscle is gained slowly, so a bigger surplus buys fat, not speed.

title: Each meal needs enough protein to trigger MPS

sources.ref: Jäger R, et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: protein and exercise. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017;14:20
Areta JL, et al. J Physiol 2013;591(9):2319–2331

body:
**Claim:** A meal needs a minimum "dose" of high-quality protein — roughly 0.4 g/kg
bodyweight, supplying enough leucine — to maximally switch on muscle protein synthesis. Very
small protein snacks under-stimulate MPS.

**Evidence:** MPS follows a dose-response to per-meal protein that saturates around
~0.4 g/kg for most people; the ISSN position stand notes leucine content and per-serving dose
drive the anabolic response (Jäger 2017; Areta 2013).

**Takeaway:** Build each of the day's protein feedings around a proper dose (for a 75 kg
person, ~30 g of quality protein) rather than grazing on tiny amounts. Prioritize
leucine-rich sources (dairy, eggs, meat, whey; or combined plant sources). The coach can use
this to explain why "I eat protein all day" can still miss the mark if no single meal clears
the threshold.

title: The 'anabolic window' is wide

sources.ref: Jäger R, et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: protein and exercise. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017;14:20
Areta JL, et al. J Physiol 2013;591(9):2319–2331

body:
**Claim:** The post-workout "anabolic window" is hours wide, not minutes. Getting a protein
meal within a few hours around training is sufficient; there is no need to rush a shake
immediately after the last set.

**Evidence:** The ISSN position stand concludes total daily protein and overall distribution
matter more than precise peri-workout timing (Jäger 2017); MPS stays elevated for many hours,
and spacing feedings across recovery works well (Areta 2013).

**Takeaway:** Prioritize hitting the daily protein target and reasonable meal spacing over
stopwatch timing. If someone trains fasted, having a protein meal within a couple of hours
before or after is plenty. The coach should redirect anxiety about immediate post-workout
timing toward the things that actually move the needle: total protein and consistency.

title: Spread protein across 3–5 meals

sources.ref: Areta JL, et al. Timing and distribution of protein ingestion during prolonged recovery from resistance exercise alters myofibrillar protein synthesis. J Physiol 2013;591(9):2319–2331
Jäger R, et al. ISSN position stand: protein and exercise. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017;14:20

body:
**Claim:** Once daily protein is adequate, distributing it across several moderate doses
(~3–5 meals of ~0.4 g/kg each) stimulates muscle protein synthesis more evenly than skewing
it into one or two large meals.

**Evidence:** Moderate protein doses every ~3 hours produced a higher, more sustained MPS
response than the same total given as fewer large or many small feedings (Areta 2013); ISSN
endorses even distribution (Jäger 2017).

**Takeaway:** Aim for roughly 3–5 protein-containing meals across the day, each with a solid
protein dose. This is a secondary optimization — total intake comes first — but it's an easy
win. The coach can suggest it to users who hit their protein total in only one or two meals.

title: Higher protein + lifting retains muscle in a deficit

sources.ref: Longland TM, et al. Higher compared with lower dietary protein during an energy deficit combined with intense exercise promotes greater lean mass gain and fat mass loss. Am J Clin Nutr 2016;103(3):738–746
Helms ER, Aragon AA, Fitschen PJ. Evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2014;11:20

body:
**Claim:** During a calorie deficit, raising protein (toward the higher end, ~2.0–2.4 g/kg
bodyweight, or higher relative to lean mass) plus continued resistance training is what
protects muscle — and can even add some — while fat is lost.

**Evidence:** A high-protein group (2.4 g/kg) gained lean mass and lost more fat than a
lower-protein group (1.2 g/kg) in a steep deficit with training (Longland 2016); Helms 2014
recommends elevating protein when dieting (up to ~2.3–3.1 g/kg of lean mass).

**Takeaway:** Cutting is the time to push protein *up*, not down, and to keep lifting heavy
so the body has a reason to keep its muscle. The coach should pair any fat-loss advice with a
higher protein target and maintained training intensity, framing them as the muscle-retention
insurance that makes a cut succeed.

title: Protein target: ~1.6–2.2 g/kg/day

sources.ref: Morton RW, et al. A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains. Br J Sports Med 2018;52(6):376–384
Jäger R, et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: protein and exercise. J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017;14:20

body:
**Claim:** To maximize muscle gain from resistance training, total daily protein intake of
roughly 1.6–2.2 g per kg of bodyweight is the key target. More than that adds little for
muscle; less leaves gains on the table.

**Evidence:** A meta-regression found the benefit of added protein plateaued around
~1.6 g/kg/day (Morton 2018); the ISSN position stand recommends ~1.4–2.0 g/kg for building
and maintaining muscle (Jäger 2017).

**Takeaway:** Hitting the daily protein target is the single most important nutrition lever
for muscle. For a user at, say, 75 kg, that's ~120–165 g/day. When the coach diagnoses stalled
growth, verifying protein intake is in range should come before tweaking training. Total
daily intake matters far more than timing or source.

title: Autoregulate load by effort (RIR/RPE)

sources.ref: Refalo MC, et al. Influence of resistance training proximity-to-failure on skeletal muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review with meta-analysis. Sports Med 2023;53(3):649–665
American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708

body:
**Claim:** Fixed loads ignore day-to-day fluctuation in strength and recovery.
Autoregulation — adjusting load or reps to hit a target effort (reps in reserve / RPE) —
matches the stimulus to how the athlete actually shows up that day.

**Evidence:** Because benefit is captured near failure rather than at a fixed %1RM (Refalo
2023), targeting an RIR band is a defensible way to prescribe effort; ACSM endorses adjusting
prescription to individual response (ACSM 2009).

**Takeaway:** Prescribe a target RIR (e.g. 2 RIR) and let the exact weight float with daily
readiness. On a strong day the user earns more reps or load at that effort; on a depleted
day they do less — automatically. This dovetails with the app's readiness signal: a
low-readiness day is exactly when autoregulation should pull load back (~12%, as the app
already does).

title: Deloads manage accumulated fatigue

sources.ref: American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708
Spiering BA, Mujika I, Sharp MA, Foulis SA. Maintaining physical performance: the minimal dose of exercise. J Strength Cond Res 2021;35(5):1449–1458

body:
**Claim:** Periodically reducing training stress (a "deload" — typically cutting volume
and/or intensity for ~1 week) lets accumulated fatigue dissipate so performance rebounds,
without meaningfully losing muscle.

**Evidence:** ACSM's framework incorporates planned variation and recovery to sustain
progression (ACSM 2009); reduced-dose training preserves strength and muscle over short
periods (Spiering 2021), so a brief deload sheds fatigue at little cost.

**Takeaway:** Program a lighter week roughly every 4–8 weeks, or when progress stalls and
fatigue markers stack up (poor sleep, elevated resting HR, dropping performance). A deload
usually means ~half the volume and slightly lighter loads, not full rest. The coach can
recommend one when the app's readiness trend and stalling e1RMs both point to fatigue rather
than under-stimulus.

title: Order exercises by priority, compounds first

sources.ref: American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708

body:
**Claim:** Performance on an exercise is best when it's done while fresh. Place the
highest-priority and most demanding (usually multi-joint/compound) movements early in the
session; whatever comes last is done under fatigue and progresses slowest.

**Evidence:** ACSM's position stand recommends sequencing large-muscle, multi-joint
exercises before smaller-muscle, single-joint ones for strength and power development (ACSM
2009).

**Takeaway:** Front-load the lifts that matter most for the user's goal — if a lagging
muscle is the priority, its key exercise can even go first, fresh, rather than defaulting to
"big compound first." This is the rule the app's workout creator already applies ("4–7
exercises in sensible order, compounds first"); the coach can justify the ordering and
adjust it to bring up a lagging muscle.

title: Muscle is maintained easily; detraining is slow

sources.ref: Spiering BA, Mujika I, Sharp MA, Foulis SA. Maintaining physical performance: the minimal dose of exercise. J Strength Cond Res 2021;35(5):1449–1458

body:
**Claim:** Maintaining strength and muscle takes far less work than building it, and short
layoffs cause little loss. Reduced-frequency, reduced-volume training preserves gains for
weeks; a missed session or two is not a setback.

**Evidence:** Strength and muscle can be maintained with as little as ~1 session/week or a
third of the usual volume, provided intensity stays reasonably high, over multi-week periods
(Spiering 2021).

**Takeaway:** Travel, illness, or a busy stretch won't erase progress — reassure users and
prescribe a minimal maintenance dose (1×/week per muscle, a few heavy sets) rather than
worrying about "losing everything." Noticeable detraining takes weeks of near-total
inactivity. The coach should treat a short adherence gap as recoverable, not cause for
panic or a program overhaul.

title: A little training goes a long way

sources.ref: Spiering BA, Mujika I, Sharp MA, Foulis SA. Maintaining physical performance: the minimal dose of exercise. J Strength Cond Res 2021;35(5):1449–1458
Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci 2017;35(11):1073–1082

body:
**Claim:** Meaningful strength and muscle can be built (and easily maintained) with
surprisingly little volume when effort is high. The first few hard sets per muscle per week
deliver most of the benefit; more adds diminishing returns.

**Evidence:** The dose-response curve is steepest at low volumes (Schoenfeld 2017), and
performance can be *maintained* on a fraction of normal training (Spiering 2021).

**Takeaway:** When time is short, don't skip — do a reduced session. A couple of hard sets
per muscle beats zero, and keeps momentum. This reframes busy weeks: the coach should offer a
trimmed but effective workout rather than an all-or-nothing plan, protecting adherence, which
is the true driver of long-term results.

title: Periodization modestly improves strength gains

sources.ref: Williams TD, Tolusso DV, Fedewa MV, Esco MR. Comparison of periodized and non-periodized resistance training on maximal strength: a meta-analysis. Sports Med 2017;47(10):2083–2100

body:
**Claim:** Planned variation of training variables over time (periodization) produces
somewhat larger maximal-strength gains than doing the same thing every session, though the
effect is modest.

**Evidence:** A meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodized programs outperformed
non-periodized ones for 1RM strength, with a small-to-moderate effect that shrank but
remained significant after adjustment (Williams 2017).

**Takeaway:** Structure beats randomness, but don't over-engineer it. A simple, coherent
plan that progresses and periodically varies intensity/volume is enough for most trainees;
elaborate periodization is not required for good results. The app's cycle + progressive
targets already provide lightweight structure — the coach should reinforce consistency and
progression over frequent program-hopping.

title: Rest 2–3 minutes on compound lifts

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, et al. Longer interset rest periods enhance muscle strength and hypertrophy in resistance-trained men. J Strength Cond Res 2016;30(7):1805–1812
American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708

body:
**Claim:** Resting ~2–3 minutes between hard sets of compound lifts produces greater
strength and hypertrophy than short (~1 min) rests, because it preserves the load and reps
you can do on the next set.

**Evidence:** In trained men, 3-minute rests beat 1-minute rests for both strength and
muscle thickness over 8 weeks (Schoenfeld 2016); ACSM recommends longer rests for
heavy/compound work (ACSM 2009).

**Takeaway:** Don't rush compounds — 2–3 min (longer for very heavy sets). Short rests
(60–90 s) are fine for isolation/machine work where recovering full performance matters less
and time efficiency helps. The app already stores a per-exercise rest target and the creator
sets `restSeconds`; the coach should default compounds to the longer end.

title: Specificity (SAID): you adapt to what you train

sources.ref: American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708
Schoenfeld BJ, Grgic J, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Strength and hypertrophy adaptations between low- vs. high-load resistance training. J Strength Cond Res 2017;31(12):3508–3523

body:
**Claim:** Adaptations are specific to the demand imposed (Specific Adaptation to Imposed
Demand). Training a movement, muscle, load range, or rep range improves that quality most.

**Evidence:** Strength gains are largest at the trained load and in the trained movement,
while hypertrophy responds across loads (Schoenfeld 2017); ACSM's framework builds
prescription around matching variables to the goal (ACSM 2009).

**Takeaway:** Programming choices must follow the goal. Want a bigger bench? The bench (and
close variations) must be trained. Want balanced physique development? Every muscle needs
direct volume, because indirect work under-stimulates lagging muscles. This underpins the
app's per-muscle volume tracking: a muscle only progresses if it receives specific,
sufficient stimulus, and the coach should reason in those terms.

title: Split design serves frequency and volume, not magic

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy. Sports Med 2016;46(11):1689–1697

body:
**Claim:** No split (full-body, upper/lower, push-pull-legs, bro-split) is inherently
superior. A split is just a way to distribute weekly volume so each muscle is trained ~2×
and each session is a recoverable size.

**Evidence:** With weekly volume equated, growth is similar across splits; the frequency
meta-analysis shows the important variable is that each muscle is hit ~2×/week, which most
sensible splits accomplish (Schoenfeld 2016).

**Takeaway:** Choose the split by training days available and recovery, not by belief in a
"best" split. 3 days → full-body or upper/lower/full; 4 → upper/lower; 5–6 → PPL or
upper/lower variants. The right split is the one that lets the user hit each muscle's weekly
range ~2× and actually adhere. The app's cycle is the container for whichever split fits.

title: Undulating vs linear periodization

sources.ref: Williams TD, Tolusso DV, Fedewa MV, Esco MR. Comparison of periodized and non-periodized resistance training on maximal strength: a meta-analysis. Sports Med 2017;47(10):2083–2100

body:
**Claim:** Both linear periodization (gradually rising intensity / falling reps over weeks)
and daily/weekly undulating periodization (varying intensity and reps more frequently) work.
Neither is clearly superior for most trainees.

**Evidence:** Meta-analysis confirms periodized > non-periodized for strength, but does not
establish a decisive winner between undulating and linear models; effects are small and
overlap (Williams 2017).

**Takeaway:** Pick the model that fits the schedule and preference, then run it
consistently. Undulating variation (e.g. a heavier lower-rep day and a lighter higher-rep
day for the same muscle within a week) pairs naturally with a rotating cycle. The coach
should not push a specific periodization brand — adherence and progression matter more than
the model label.

title: Warm up with ramp-up sets, not just the target

sources.ref: American College of Sports Medicine. Progression models in resistance training for healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2009;41(3):687–708

body:
**Claim:** A brief, specific warm-up — light general movement plus a few ascending "ramp-up"
sets of the first exercise — primes performance and reduces injury risk, without adding
meaningful fatigue. Warm-up sets are not counted as working volume.

**Evidence:** ACSM's guidance includes appropriate warm-up as part of safe, effective
resistance-training prescription (ACSM 2009); ramp-up sets rehearse the movement and prepare
tissues at the working load.

**Takeaway:** Before a heavy compound, do 2–4 progressively heavier sets up to the working
weight; isolation and lighter machine work usually need only 1 light set or none. This is
why the app logs warm-up sets separately and excludes them from volume and e1RM stats — they
prepare you for the working sets that actually count.

title: Per-muscle weekly volume landmarks

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci 2017;35(11):1073–1082
Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy. Sports Med 2016;46(11):1689–1697

body:
**Claim:** Each muscle has a rough weekly-set range that is productive: below it you
under-stimulate, inside it you get most of the growth, and above it you accumulate fatigue
faster than benefit. These landmarks differ by muscle.

**Evidence:** The dose-response and frequency meta-analyses (Schoenfeld 2016, 2017) support
per-muscle set ranges distributed across ~2 sessions/week; popularized as MEV/MAV/MRV
("minimum effective", "maximum adaptive", "maximum recoverable" volume) landmarks.

**Takeaway:** Program to each muscle's range rather than a single global number — bigger
muscles and those getting heavy indirect work (e.g. front delts from pressing) need fewer
*direct* sets. This is exactly what the app encodes in `MUSCLE_RANGES`, with per-muscle
`[lo, hi]` pairs; the coach should treat those bounds as the target and reason per muscle.

title: Soreness is a poor recovery gauge

sources.ref: Damas F, et al. Resistance training-induced changes in integrated myofibrillar protein synthesis are related to hypertrophy only after attenuation of muscle damage. J Physiol 2016;594(18):5209–5222

body:
**Claim:** Delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is a weak, unreliable indicator of recovery
or of whether a muscle is ready to train. You can be sore yet recovered, or recovered yet
still growing without soreness.

**Evidence:** Soreness tracks unaccustomed stimulus and muscle damage, which is dissociated
from the hypertrophic adaptation itself (Damas 2016); it fades as a muscle adapts to a
given exercise regardless of ongoing progress.

**Takeaway:** Don't use soreness to decide whether to train. Better readiness signals are
performance (can you hit your numbers?), sleep, energy, and resting-HR trend — the inputs the
app's readiness record actually uses. The coach should redirect users who gauge recovery by
soreness toward these more meaningful markers.

title: Elevated resting heart rate flags under-recovery

sources.ref: Manresa-Rocamora A, et al. Heart rate variability-guided training ... Int J Environ Res Public Health 2021;18(19):10237

body:
**Claim:** A resting heart rate meaningfully above one's 7-day baseline (roughly ≥5 bpm) can
indicate accumulated fatigue, poor sleep, illness, or life stress — a reason to ease off
that day.

**Evidence:** Resting HR and HRV are complementary autonomic markers; deviations from
individual baseline are used in the monitoring literature to flag under-recovery
(Manresa-Rocamora 2021 and the broader autonomic-monitoring evidence).

**Takeaway:** Judge resting HR against the person's own rolling baseline, not a population
number. The app already encodes this: `rhrElevated` flags a resting HR ≥5 bpm over the
7-day average, and that flag feeds the low-readiness assessment. The coach should read an
elevated-RHR day as a cue to reduce intensity or check for illness — especially if sleep is
also poor.

title: HRV as a recovery and readiness marker

sources.ref: Manresa-Rocamora A, et al. Heart rate variability-guided training for enhancing cardiac-vagal modulation, aerobic fitness, and endurance performance: a systematic review with meta-analysis. Int J Environ Res Public Health 2021;18(19):10237

body:
**Claim:** Overnight heart-rate variability (HRV) reflects autonomic recovery state and can
help guide training load. Higher-than-usual HRV suggests readiness; a suppressed reading
suggests accumulated stress or under-recovery — but only relative to the individual's own
baseline and trend.

**Evidence:** HRV-guided training can match or modestly outperform fixed plans by steering
hard sessions toward recovered days, though effects are moderate and methodology-dependent
(Manresa-Rocamora 2021).

**Takeaway:** Use HRV as a *trend* against personal baseline, never as a single-day verdict —
it's noisy and affected by alcohol, illness, and measurement conditions. This is why the
app's readiness record combines the watch's HRV/readiness score with sleep and a manual
energy check-in rather than relying on HRV alone. The coach should weigh the whole readiness
picture.

title: Life stress competes with training recovery

sources.ref: Craven J, et al. Effects of acute sleep loss on physical performance: a systematic and meta-analytical review. Sports Med 2022;52(11):2669–2690

body:
**Claim:** Psychological and lifestyle stress (work pressure, poor sleep, under-eating) draw
on the same recovery capacity as training. High life stress slows recovery and adaptation,
so training tolerance drops during stressful periods.

**Evidence:** Sleep loss — a common consequence and correlate of stress — directly impairs
performance and increases perceived effort (Craven 2022); stress and recovery share
physiological pathways in the broader monitoring literature.

**Takeaway:** When life is chaotic, expect lower training capacity and program more
conservatively — hold volume steady or reduce it rather than pushing PRs. The app's manual
energy check-in and note field capture this subjective load; the coach should factor a run of
low-energy/high-stress days into recommendations, not just the objective watch metrics.

title: Adjust today's session to today's readiness

sources.ref: Craven J, et al. Effects of acute sleep loss on physical performance. Sports Med 2022;52(11):2669–2690
Manresa-Rocamora A, et al. Heart rate variability-guided training ... Int J Environ Res Public Health 2021;18(19):10237

body:
**Claim:** Recovery fluctuates day to day, so a fixed plan will sometimes ask for more than
the body can give. Trimming intensity/volume on a clearly under-recovered day preserves the
training stimulus while avoiding digging a deeper fatigue hole.

**Evidence:** Sleep loss lowers performance capacity for that day (Craven 2022), and
readiness-guided approaches steer hard work toward recovered days for better outcomes
(Manresa-Rocamora 2021).

**Takeaway:** On a low-readiness day (poor sleep, low energy, or elevated resting HR),
reduce load ~10–15% or shift the hard session — don't force it. The app operationalizes this
with a single low-readiness rule (`isLowReadiness`) that trims intensity ~12%; the coach's
day-to-day advice should agree with that assessment rather than override it.

title: Muscles need ~24–72 h between hard sessions

sources.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy. Sports Med 2016;46(11):1689–1697
Damas F, et al. J Physiol 2016;594(18):5209–5222

body:
**Claim:** After a hard session a muscle needs roughly 24–72 hours to recover and complete
its adaptive response before being trained hard again. This recovery window is what sets a
practical ceiling on useful frequency.

**Evidence:** Elevated muscle protein synthesis persists for ~1–2 days post-training (Damas
2016), and ~2×/week frequency (i.e. ~48–72 h spacing) supports growth well (Schoenfeld
2016).

**Takeaway:** Don't train the same muscle hard on back-to-back days; leave ~48 h between
sessions for a given muscle. This is why sensible splits alternate muscle emphasis across
days. When scheduling the cycle, the coach should avoid stacking, say, two heavy pressing
days in a row and instead interleave push/pull/legs so each muscle gets its recovery window.

title: Sleep loss degrades training performance

sources.ref: Craven J, et al. Effects of acute sleep loss on physical performance: a systematic and meta-analytical review. Sports Med 2022;52(11):2669–2690

body:
**Claim:** Short or poor sleep measurably impairs physical performance — including strength,
power, and high-intensity work — and raises perceived effort, so a hard session on little
sleep is both harder and less productive.

**Evidence:** A meta-analytical review found acute sleep loss reduces physical performance
across multiple domains, with next-day and afternoon sessions particularly affected (Craven
2022).

**Takeaway:** Treat a bad night as a real signal, not an excuse to ignore. On low-sleep
days, expect lower output and either reduce intensity/volume or swap to easier work — which
is exactly what the app's low-readiness rule does (it trims intensity ~12% when sleep is
poor). The coach should connect a poor-sleep readiness record to a lighter recommendation
for that day.

title: More sleep improves performance

sources.ref: Mah CD, Mah KE, Kezirian EJ, Dement WC. The effects of sleep extension on the athletic performance of collegiate basketball players. Sleep 2011;34(7):943–950

body:
**Claim:** Extending sleep toward ~8–10 hours improves athletic performance — speed,
accuracy, reaction time, and mood — in people who were previously under-slept. Sleep is a
performance enhancer, not just damage control.

**Evidence:** When collegiate basketball players extended sleep for several weeks, sprint
times, shooting accuracy, reaction time, and mood all improved significantly (Mah 2011).

**Takeaway:** For someone chasing progress, prioritizing sleep is one of the
highest-leverage, lowest-cost interventions available — often more impactful than tweaking
the program. The coach should treat consistently short sleep (visible in the app's readiness
history) as a first-order thing to address before adding training volume.
